does: This paper proposes Preference Delta Aggregation (PDA), a framework that learns from weak preference signals by extracting relative quality differences from paired outputs of smaller models, converting each signal into a LoRA adapter through preference optimization, then aggregating multiple adapters via LoRA merging. The key innovation is Geometric Alignment Merging (GAM), which aligns adapter subspaces before merging to reduce directional interference and enable more robust composition of diverse preference deltas. The method is evaluated on knowledge reasoning and agentic search benchmarks, demonstrating that multiple weak signals can be constructively combined to improve larger language models.
connects: This work builds on recent findings that weak preference signals from model pairs with limited individual quality can provide useful supervision, extending prior research on learning from imperfect feedback. It combines preference optimization techniques commonly used in alignment with LoRA adaptation and merging, which have emerged as efficient methods for combining model knowledge. The geometric alignment approach responds to known challenges in multi-adapter merging where conflicting directions between adapters degrade performance, drawing on insights from manifold alignment and subspace-based composition methods developed in transfer learning and multi-task learning literature.
enables: This framework opens possibilities for training strong models using abundant cheap supervision from weaker model pairs rather than scarce high-quality annotations, potentially reducing the data bottleneck in LLM development. The geometric alignment merging technique could enable more general multi-source adapter composition beyond preference learning, applicable to combining knowledge from diverse fine-tuning objectives or domains. Future work might explore automatic methods for selecting which weak signals to aggregate, investigating theoretical properties of preference delta composition, or extending the approach to integrate signals from heterogeneous model families and sizes.
